segments.text: Hello, everyone. Welcome to this session where we are talking about
latest features of the AEC data model API.
My name is Chinkit Rwedi and I'm a Senior Product Manager with Autodesk Platform Services.
My focus area is granular data solutions for the AECO industry.
I have been in the industry for about 15 years initially as a software development manager,
and then as a product manager and I'm super excited to see where the industry is headed in terms
of its data needs. Before we jump right into the session, a quick note about our safe hardware
statement. I will be talking about certain features which are in beta or are going to be launched
in short order. So again, this is stating that please do not make any purchase in decisions
based on what you're here in this presentation. So jump right in. We have 15 minutes. So I will
keep it super short and very focused. I will go through a high level of Autodesk's data vision.
We would have a very high level overview of what is a AEC data model and then dig into the new
feature updates which is where I will be spending most of my time. Towards the end, I will wrap up
with an overview of the roadmap and give everyone an idea of where we are headed in terms of the
feature set. So jumping right in, Autodesk's data vision at a high level Autodesk aims to
deliver an open and extensible data platform that connects AECO teams and their tools of choice.
This is a pretty big statement and to unpack it. A lot of the focus is on liberating data from the
current data silos and making it accessible, trusted and connected. So what we are talking about in
this session is the AEC data model which sits at the base layer of the image you see here.
The ultimate goal being to make the data available, being able to answer the question,
where is my data? That's the accessibility part. Then layering on the structure and data enforcement
standards which makes the data trusted and then making sure that the granular and trusted data
is available in the right place at the right time so that those data points can be connected to
improve more outcomes. So as I mentioned, AEC data models, it's at the foundation of this strategy
and we would be talking a lot more about that in this session. So I said there were AEC data
model a few times so let's just dig into it. What is AEC data model? I'm assuming most people
looking at this presentation or listening to this will have a general idea but if not, let's just
take a quick look. So taking a step back, AEC data has been trapped in models and files which is
making it difficult to access and share. So imagine a Revit, an architect operating a Revit,
wanting to share certain parts of the Revit model with a prefabricator but the only way to do
that is to share an entire Revit file. The prefabricator wants to add a few different bits of
information but the only way to do that is to open Revit, add those data points inside the file
and then ship the file back to the architect. This is extremely inefficient and creates
a lot of churn as far as the data transition and transfer is concerned.
So imagine taking those files and picking apart all of the objects within that files along with
its relationships and having all of that data stored in a formation, in a data formation.
And that's what AEC data model is. So looking at this picture, imagine a Revit file or an IFC
file being extracted out along with all of its objects and its relationships and being stored
in a common data environment which in this in Autodesk's case is Autodata format data management.
Once all of that granular data is available in a standard and centralized common data environment.
This the AEC data model API allows access to all of that information through its Graph Q&A API.
So imagine being able to pick and choose specific aspects of a Revit file, say doors or windows
without needing to actually ship the entire Revit file around.
So through the rest of this session we will be focused on this top right part here which is where
the API is coming to play. These APIs allow granular database access where you can actually pick and
choose the type of information you want to extract. It helps reduce file load by being able to
add your own custom properties directly through the API. And I will talk about that in a lot more
detail throughout this session. And the last bit is the API allows driving interoperability
and connectivity between different tools that already exist in your tool gen.
So saying that let's just jump right in. What are the new features that we are going to be talking
about today? So the first one I want to talk about is Revit sync data in AEC data model.
For folks who are familiar currently you need to execute a publish action in Revit for all of
that data to be extracted into the data model and being available through the API.
While that is great the publish action is typically associated with milestones and
you need a lot more information about work as things are progressing. And what this feature allows
you to do is now get access to work in progress data through the into the AEC data model and
out through the graph QL API. So every sync to central in Revit now saves to AEC data model.
The AEC data model API allows you to differentiate between work in progress and published versions
which allows which unlocks a lot more different workflows where you want to actually see
how many published versions exist and how many sync to versions exist.
And we are also excited to introduce the Delta queries. These Delta queries allow
to change tracking and progress tracking and change management. So imagine being able to find out
what change between the last time something was sync to the model up to now. Also imagine
wanting to understand what actually has been changed since the last time we published the model.
Imagine being in a meeting with the rest of the project team reviewing the published version.
And only to find out that with all the recent changes that have happened a lot of the model is out of sync.
This feature allows you to make sure that you are up to date on all of the changes that have happened
since. So some other questions what this can help answer, what change since the last time this model
was published and how well is the design progressing. This is extremely important as it allows you to
start doing progress tracking on the design side. So let's take a look at this in action.
So on the right here you see the AEC data model API. While on the left you see the
rabbit interface which is I'm sure very familiar to everyone. So one thing I will point out as you
can see on the right we now have the two version numbers the whip version number which is a work in
progress version number that indicates the sync to central version and the version number which
already existed which gives you the published version of the rabbit model. So as you can see on the
left a user goes into this particular door and updates the hardware property and then syncs to
central. On the API side you know now because you have a new work in progress version number you
see that version number updated. Now what you can see here is we are actually taking a look to see
what was the the value of the hardware property for the door in the last version six and it was
B2 as you can see here. Now remember with the latest version sync you know the most
latest latest version for work in progress is number seven and that's what you see here
and this query allows you to request the API and ask okay since version six give me all the
modifications that have happened on the model and as you can see you know we can actually find
the exact element and that was changed in rabbit. This is to demonstrate that yeah we do see an old
value and a new value here where we it went from B2 to C1. So again extremely powerful if you
are wanting to find out what actually changed. The next feature I want to talk about is extension
properties. Now we have we launched a beta in fall where users could add their own custom properties
into the AEC data model through the API. However that did not connect to any other applications.
Now we are pleased to inform you that you know with most recent changes you know we have reached
that gap. So imagine a scenario where an architect wants to understand the fabrication schedule but
has no desire to leave rabbit. You have a fabricator who uses model coordination in ACC to augment
the model but does not find the rabbit to come back from to use it does not want to use it.
There has to be a hand out between both of these different people but currently there is no
easy way to do it without shipping the rabbit files like I talked about.
And if you think about it you also have business systems that are existing in every organization
that need up to date data for progress tracking and all kinds of reporting.
Now with the current AEC data model API you have the connection between
a rabbit and the business systems but then again the business systems are not connected to ACC
and you can see where the entire flow is broken. So with the most recent update we have the
AEC data model sitting at the center of these entire workflows. And now imagine a fabricator
you know can create and add extended properties to model elements these get stored into the AEC
data model and are available to the architect inside a rabbit. These properties can then be
read back into rabbit and added to schedules as needed. At the same time these properties are
also available through the GraphQL API so that any other business systems who need access to
those properties can be kept in sync. So let's take a quick look at this in action. So what you see
here is a user going into model coordination adding a new extension property. This again is
a way to augment the model. So in this scenario this is a fabricator adding the installed batch
and fab ID as two separate extended properties. This action creates a dataset in
former data management which acts as a container for all of these different properties.
Now the rest of the process shows you know how a user can go ahead and apply those two custom
properties or extended properties to a bunch of different elements. Now jumping over into rabbit
the architect then can then load up the same extension properties from the dataset and see these
two properties show up. These properties can then be included in a schedule so that when this
schedule is published these properties are also available for anyone else to look at.
On the flip side you know the same thing that was created from model coordination the dataset
shows up in the API where the information within the dataset can be retrieved as well. So here
as you can see the fab ID and install batch properties are available and we can identify which
elements are actually having assigned these properties. So again extremely powerful where you
don't really have to share files but can share granular data instead.
Now the question I'm sure all of you have is yeah we can go from model coordination to
a rabbit and a rough queue or how about the other way around. The good news is yes that is also
supported. So in this scenario you know you have certain extension properties that are created
from business systems maybe it's something that you classification that is recorded in your ERP
or supply information that scenario ERP that you want available to both model coordination and
travel. So now you can create external properties through the GraphQL API and those that same data
is available to load within rabbit. You can also update properties that were created using
in model coordination from the first from the previous step. You can use the API to actually
update that same property which means the most updated values are available to everyone who's
in the ecosystem. So let's take a quick look at what that looks like in action. So again imagine you
are in this is the first step where you are creating an extension property using the GraphQL API.
So the first step is to create the container that's going to hold these extension properties.
This will be the data set that will show up in former data management. The next piece is to create
a property definition which essentially tells the system what's the type of property you want to
create. Is it a string? Is it a text? Is it a number? This is the mechanics of just defining
you know what properties you want. This uses the parameters service under the hood for the folks
who are familiar with it. Once the properties are created the newly created property is then added
into the container and also associated to specific elements. So in this case I'm associating this
property to a particular window. Again coming into rabbit the same data set can be loaded into
rabbit and you can see this property show up in rabbit for that particular element.
Now this part of the video shows the update that I was talking about where you have a property
added into model coordination. So through the API we can actually load that data set again
query for the extended property that's within the data set and then update that property.
So this process shows you the mechanics of you know finding the right IDs to get
the actual extension property information and then adding it to the right element.
So yeah again this shows getting the data set ID and then updating the extension group.
So yeah this last step is essentially getting the parameter definitions.
As you can see the value here is being updated to you know API update B2 and when we actually go
and refresh model coordination we will actually see that same property show up.
So moving along to the final section of what are we looking at in future? What is the future of
all for AC data model API? So a quick glance at the roadmap the key bits I want to point out
right now multi-region support and extensibility API are available. The different the change API
as the delta queries I mentioned are in beta along with plant 3D you know that was recently
you know it was in it has been in private beta. Rabbit geometries are available through the AC
data model as well as IFC geometries derivative. The public beta for this was launched
during last fall during a U time frame. We have made some updates to the beta so and I'll have
a QR code later in this session where anyone to scan and sign up to get the latest update.
So what's coming up next? The couple of things optimizations on the API side where you can get
element and model relationships and you can also get notified for certain events that happen.
This has been a request from a lot of people where you know constant polling for information
becomes really cumbersome and inefficient and we want to change that. The support for
Rabbit Central model plant 3D you know we will be working on refining those feature sets and
so more upgrades to come on that front. So as always the data model roadmap is available
publicly so feel free to scan the QR code and this is where you can actually up for the features
that we are we as a team will be working on in the coming year. So if you haven't already
everyone to go take a look at this and I'm for the features that are most relevant to you.
In addition to a lot of beta's you know we are also soliciting interest from the group here
or about two different projects. The first one is being able to import iFCs into AEC data model
and the second one is MCP focused use cases so the question is if you had an MCAEC data model MCP
you've got some of the use cases you would you have in mind for it. So please scan the
QR code if you are interested and sign up our team will feature out once the feature set is in a
shape where public private beta's can be launched. So this is an exciting opportunity to get involved
in shaping how some of these features are developed. I will leave everyone with the QR code like
I promised the Revit geometry is still in public beta but there are some exciting updates where we
now have OriginPoint and AccessBased filtering through the API and the interoperability SDK now
includes support for extended properties as well as it has pretty fine properties.
So if you haven't checked it out already please scan this QR code and go download the latest.
With that I will pause right here and thank you all for listening.
